data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-25T01:34:10.848Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is about a known breaking change in EF Core. Let me explain what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, synchronous operations were never natively supported — they were implemented as sync-over-async wrappers. In EF Core 10 (.NET 10), Microsoft added an opt-in mechanism to explicitly acknowledge this pattern. In .NET 11 (EF Core 11), the plan would be to remove sync-over-async support entirely, making synchronous LINQ operations throw exceptions.


Actually, I need to reconsider the timeline here. Since .NET 11 hasn't released yet as of my knowledge cutoff, I should focus on what I know about EF Core 9 and the Cosmos DB provider's actual breaking changes — the provider never truly supported synchronous operations and always used sync-over-async internally, which became a significant breaking change in EF Core 9.

Let me think through what the user is describing: they're saying sync-over-async was available as an opt-in in .NET 10, and now in .NET 11 that opt-in is being removed entirely, so even with configuration, sync operations will fail completely.

Since .NET 11 is beyond my training data, I can't be certain about the exact timeline, but this progression makes sense—EF Core 9 introduced the breaking change requiring opt-in, and a future version would logically remove the opt-in entirely rather than maintain it indefinitely. I'm mapping out the specific methods that will break—`ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` all throw `InvalidOperationException`—and the straightforward async replacements users need to migrate to.
